---
title: "Phase 1 Capstone Project"
subtitle: "Linear Algebra Visualization Suite"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    geometry:
      - margin=0.85in
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
    number-sections: false
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{amssymb}
      \usepackage{amsmath}
      \usepackage{tikz}
      \usepackage{enumitem}
      \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, calc}
      \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=1.5em}
      \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
---

\begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Time estimate} & \~{}3 hours \\
\textbf{Language} & Python (NumPy + matplotlib) \\
\textbf{Output} & 3 figures + numerical verification \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{1em}

Build a visualization suite that makes every Phase 1 concept visible: see how matrices transform space, watch eigenvectors hold their direction, and decompose any transformation into the SVD pipeline.

# Part 1 --- Matrix Transformation Visualizer (\~1h)

**Goal:** Show what a matrix \emph{does} to a geometric object.

## 1a. Unit circle transformation

Pick the matrix:
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0.5 & 1.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

- Plot the unit circle ($100+$ points on $\cos\theta, \sin\theta$)
- Apply $A$ to every point: $\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}$
- Plot the resulting ellipse on the same axes
- Add the standard basis vectors $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2$ and their images $A\mathbf{e}_1, A\mathbf{e}_2$ as arrows

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, scale=0.8]
  % Unit circle
  \draw[blue!60, thick] (0,0) circle (1.5);
  \node[blue!60, font=\footnotesize] at (1.2, 1.2) {unit circle};

  % Ellipse (transformed)
  \draw[red!70, thick, rotate=20] (0,0) ellipse (2.5 and 1.0);
  \node[red!70, font=\footnotesize] at (2.2, -0.8) {$A$ applied};

  % Basis vectors
  \draw[->, thick, blue!80] (0,0) -- (1.5, 0) node[below, font=\footnotesize] {$\mathbf{e}_1$};
  \draw[->, thick, blue!80] (0,0) -- (0, 1.5) node[left, font=\footnotesize] {$\mathbf{e}_2$};

  % Transformed basis
  \draw[->, thick, red!80] (0,0) -- (2.2, 0.5) node[right, font=\footnotesize] {$A\mathbf{e}_1$};
  \draw[->, thick, red!80] (0,0) -- (0.8, 1.8) node[above, font=\footnotesize] {$A\mathbf{e}_2$};

  % Axes
  \draw[gray!50, ->] (-2,0) -- (3,0);
  \draw[gray!50, ->] (0,-1.5) -- (0,2.5);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

## 1b. Eigenvector overlay

Compute the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ and eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2$ of $A$ using `numpy.linalg.eig`. On the same figure:

- Draw $\mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2$ as dashed arrows from the origin
- Draw $A\mathbf{v}_1 = \lambda_1 \mathbf{v}_1$ and $A\mathbf{v}_2 = \lambda_2 \mathbf{v}_2$ as solid arrows
- Label each with its eigenvalue

The eigenvectors should visually confirm: \emph{they don't rotate, they only stretch.}

## 1c. Determinant and area

Compute $\det(A)$. Draw a unit square (vertices at origin, $\mathbf{e}_1$, $\mathbf{e}_2$, $\mathbf{e}_1 + \mathbf{e}_2$) and its image under $A$. The area of the parallelogram should equal $|\det(A)|$.

Print: ``det(A) = {:.3f}, parallelogram area = {:.3f}`` and verify they match.

# Part 2 --- SVD Decomposition Walkthrough (\~1h)

**Goal:** Decompose $A = U \Sigma V^T$ and show each step visually.

## 2a. Compute and display SVD

Use `numpy.linalg.svd` to get $U$, $\Sigma$, $V^T$. Print all three matrices. Verify $A = U \Sigma V^T$ numerically (should match to $< 10^{-14}$).

## 2b. Step-by-step transformation figure

Create a **4-panel** figure showing the SVD pipeline applied to the unit circle:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, scale=0.55, every node/.style={font=\small}]
  % Panel 1: unit circle
  \draw[blue!60, thick] (0,0) circle (1);
  \node[below] at (0, -1.4) {Original};
  \node[below, font=\footnotesize] at (0, -1.9) {unit circle};

  % Arrow
  \draw[->, thick] (1.5, 0) -- (3, 0) node[midway, above, font=\footnotesize] {$V^T$};

  % Panel 2: rotated circle
  \begin{scope}[xshift=4.5cm]
    \draw[green!60!black, thick] (0,0) circle (1);
    \node[below] at (0, -1.4) {Step 1};
    \node[below, font=\footnotesize] at (0, -1.9) {rotate by $V^T$};
  \end{scope}

  % Arrow
  \draw[->, thick] (6, 0) -- (7.5, 0) node[midway, above, font=\footnotesize] {$\Sigma$};

  % Panel 3: stretched ellipse
  \begin{scope}[xshift=9cm]
    \draw[orange, thick] (0,0) ellipse (1.8 and 0.6);
    \node[below] at (0, -1.4) {Step 2};
    \node[below, font=\footnotesize] at (0, -1.9) {scale by $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$};
  \end{scope}

  % Arrow
  \draw[->, thick] (11.3, 0) -- (12.8, 0) node[midway, above, font=\footnotesize] {$U$};

  % Panel 4: final ellipse
  \begin{scope}[xshift=14.3cm]
    \draw[red!70, thick, rotate=25] (0,0) ellipse (1.8 and 0.6);
    \node[below] at (0, -1.4) {Step 3};
    \node[below, font=\footnotesize] at (0, -1.9) {rotate by $U$};
  \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

For each panel, transform the unit circle points by the cumulative product of matrices up to that step. The final panel should match your ellipse from Part 1.

## 2c. Singular values and condition number

- The semi-axes of the final ellipse have lengths $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$
- Print the condition number $\kappa = \sigma_1 / \sigma_2$
- Explain in a comment: what would $\kappa = 1$ look like? What about $\kappa = 1000$?

# Part 3 --- Numerical Verification (\~1h)

**Goal:** Confirm core identities computationally.

## 3a. Rank, null space, and column space

For the matrix:
$$B = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$$

- Compute rank via SVD (count nonzero singular values above a tolerance)
- Find a basis for the null space (columns of $V$ corresponding to zero singular values)
- Verify that $B \mathbf{n} = \mathbf{0}$ for each null space vector $\mathbf{n}$
- Verify rank-nullity: $\mathrm{rank}(B) + \mathrm{nullity}(B) = 3$

## 3b. Projection matrix

Build the projection matrix onto the column space of $B$:
$$P = B (B^T B)^+ B^T$$
where $(B^T B)^+$ is the pseudoinverse. Verify:

- $P^2 = P$ (idempotent)
- $P^T = P$ (symmetric)
- $P\mathbf{b}_i = \mathbf{b}_i$ for each column of $B$ (columns project to themselves)

## 3c. Eigenvalue-determinant-trace relationships

For the original matrix $A$:

- Verify $\det(A) = \lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2$
- Verify $\mathrm{tr}(A) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$
- Verify $\sigma_i^2 = \lambda_i(A^T A)$

Print each pair of values and the relative error.

# Deliverables

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \texttt{transform\_and\_eigen.png} --- unit circle + ellipse + eigenvectors + area
  \item \texttt{svd\_pipeline.png} --- 4-panel SVD decomposition
  \item Printed numerical verification of all identities (rank-nullity, projection, det/trace)
\end{enumerate}
